coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots assisted CuCo2S4/MWCNT nanoflakes as superior bifunctional electrocatalysts for dye-sensitized solar cell and supercapacitor applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Gayathri, V.
Praveen, E.
Jayakumar, K.
Karazhanov, S.
Mohan, C. Raja
coredata.dc:description: In this investigation, Graphene Quantum Dot (GQD) and MWCNT embedded Copper Cobalt Sulfide (CCS) composites for energy conversion and energy storage performance have been investigated. The hydrothermally synthesized bare CCS and their composites with MWCNT and GQD have been characterized for XRD, SEM, TEM, and XPS to investigate the crystalline nature, shape, and chemical content of the synthesized composites. The electrochemical properties of CCS, CCS/MWCNT and CCS/MWCNT/GQD composites have been analyzed using cyclic voltammogram (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The CV spectrum results demonstrate that the GQD embedded CCS/MWCNT composite has a lower peak-to-peak potential separation (EPP) and a higher peak current density (JPC) than CCS and CCS/MWNCT composites, indicating significant electrochemical activity and electrical conductivity. The estimated power conversion efficiency (PCE) of the CCS, CCS/MWCNT, and CCS/MWCNT/GQD composites have been determined in the following order: CCS/MWCNT/GQD (4.15%) > CCS/MWCNT (3.09%)> CCS (1.91%). In addition, the prepared CCS, CCS/MWCNT and CCS/MWCNT/GQD composites are used as a working electrode to investigate energy storage performance. Among the CCS-based composites, the GQD enriched CCS/MWCNT electrode exhibits the maximum effective specific capacity (402 C g−1) with 70% capacitive retention after 1200 charge-discharge cycles. As a result, we led to the realization that the GQD-supported CCS microstructure is an acceptable electrode material for integrated energy conversion and storage gadgets.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Supercapacitors
DSSC
Nanostructures
Energy
Bi metal sulfides

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of working electrode for supercapacitor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank BT corp for providing MWCNT (CAS No: 308068-56-6).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD spectra of CCS, CCS/MWCNT and CCS/MWCNT/GQD composites.

The SEM micrograph of (a) CCS, (b) CCS/MWCNT, (c) CCS/MWCNT/GQD composites; The TEM images of (d,f) CCS/MWCNT/GQD, (e) lattice pattern of CCS/MWCNT/GQD and (g) SAED pattern of CCCS/MWCNT/GQD composite.
The XPS spectra of (a) Co 2p (b) Cu 2p (c) S 2p (d) C 1 s and (e) O 1 s levels of the CCS/MWCNT/GQD composite.
(a) The cyclic voltammetry with scan rate at 50 mVs−1 (b) Tafel slope of CCS, CCS/MWCNT and CCS/MWCNT/GQD CEs respectively.
(a) J-V curve and (b) Nyquist plot of CCS, CCS/MWCNT and CCS/MWCNT/GQD CEs respectively.
IPCE spectra of CCS, CCS/MWCNT and CCS/MWCNT/GQD CEs.

(a) The CV spectra of CCS, CCS/MWCNT and CCS/MWCNT/GQD at 20 mVs−1 (b) CV spectra of CCS/MWCNT/GQD at different scan rates (c) The GCD spectra of CCS, CCS/MWCNT and CCS/MWCNT/GQD at 2 Ag−1 (d) The GCD spectra of CCS/MWCNT/GQD at different specific current (e) The Nyquist spectra of CCS, CCS/MWCNT and CCS/MWCNT/GQD and (f) stability test of CCS/MWCNT/GQ at 20 Ag−1.
(a) CV curve, (b) GCD spectra, (c) rangone plot and (d) Nyquist plot of the asymmetric supercapacitor device.
Functional parameters estimated from J-V spectra and electrochemical studies.
Comparison of electrochemical performance with recently published ternary metal sulfide-based works.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots assisted CuCo2S4/MWCNT nanoflakes as superior bifunctional electrocatalysts for dye-sensitized solar cell and supercapacitor applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this investigation, Graphene Quantum Dot (GQD) and MWCNT embedded Copper Cobalt Sulfide (CCS) composites for energy conversion and energy storage performance have been investigated. The hydrothermally synthesized bare CCS and their composites with MWCNT and GQD have been characterized for XRD, SEM, TEM, and XPS to investigate the crystalline nature, shape, and chemical content of the synthesized composites. The electrochemical properties of CCS, CCS/MWCNT and CCS/MWCNT/GQD composites have been analyzed using cyclic voltammogram (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The CV spectrum results demonstrate that the GQD embedded CCS/MWCNT composite has a lower peak-to-peak potential separation (EPP) and a higher peak current density (JPC) than CCS and CCS/MWNCT composites, indicating significant electrochemical activity and electrical conductivity. The estimated power conversion efficiency (PCE) of the CCS, CCS/MWCNT, and CCS/MWCNT/GQD composites have been determined in the following order: CCS/MWCNT/GQD (4.15%) > CCS/MWCNT (3.09%)> CCS (1.91%). In addition, the prepared CCS, CCS/MWCNT and CCS/MWCNT/GQD composites are used as a working electrode to investigate energy storage performance. Among the CCS-based composites, the GQD enriched CCS/MWCNT electrode exhibits the maximum effective specific capacity (402 C g−1) with 70% capacitive retention after 1200 charge-discharge cycles. As a result, we led to the realization that the GQD-supported CCS microstructure is an acceptable electrode material for integrated energy conversion and storage gadgets.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
The energy crises and pollution produced by reliance on fossil fuels are the most significant obstacles to building a low-carbon economy that can be sustained. The advent of smart materials that can perform a variety of tasks, from energy harvesting to storage, has been anxiously anticipated by researchers [1,2]. The depletion of fossil fuels primarily drives the escalating energy and environmental crises and has hastened the development of Dye-Sensitized Solar Cells (DSSCs), which are distinguished by their low cost, ease of production, high efficiency, and environmental friendliness [3]. Despite extensive research, commercialization of the technology has been hampered by its relatively high cost and lack of stability. The Counter Electrode (CE) is critical in the DSSC because it collects external electrons and electrocatalytically reduces the electrolyte from oxidation to a reduction state (from
I
3
−
to
I
−
) [4]. This typical electrocatalytic reduction process necessitates several fundamental processes, comprising electrolyte diffusion, adsorption, catalytic reduction, and desorption [5]. Likewise, diffusion of the electrolyte requires electrocatalytic materials with suitable nanostructures that allow the electrolyte to spread out quickly. Hence, electrocatalytic materials with high conductivity and electrocatalytic activity will be essential for the subsequent processes [6]. Platinum (Pt) has typically been employed as a catalyst for CE in DSSCs. Owing to its constraints and limited supply, the technology may not even be mass-produced on a large scale [7,8]. Furthermore, Pt precursors require high-temperature sintering (∼450 °C), limiting the use of Pt catalysts on flexible substrates. Moreover, in a corrosive electrolyte, atomic Pt can corrode, impacting the long-term stability and performance of the device. As a result, substituting Pt with robust Pt-free CE is quite desirable, as it reduces the cost of the DSSC[9]. A variety of materials have been investigated in recent research to replace Pt, such as carbonaceous materials, conducting polymers, transition metal sulfides (TMS)[10], oxides [11,12], selenides [13], carbides [14,15] and nitrides [16,17] etc. TMS has reached the most attention because of its plentiful supply, variety of phases, structures, chemical composites, high electrical conductivity, and good electrocatalytic activity. Since the transition metals are so versatile and have such good catalytic activity for I3 - reduction, such that, binary transition metal sulfides like CoS, MoS2, Sb2S3, and many others have drawn more and more interest[18–23]. Compared to binary metal sulfides, ternary metal sulfides have been identified as a highly promising CE due to the mixture of two cations classes, which leads to attractive structures, a suitable bandgap structure, and rapid redox reactions. Due to the coexistence of two different cations, ternary transition metal sulfides have recently been discovered to be highly advantageous for promising CEs, providing exciting morphology, abundant redox reactions, switchable bandgap structure, and electronic and optical properties by modifying the composition proportion [24,25]. Recent research on Ni1−XMoXS [26], CoMoS4 [27], and NiCo2S4 [28] has revealed new information about their catalytic activities for the
I
3
−
reduction reaction, as well as their potential application in DSSC CEs. Bakhytzhan Baptayev et al.[29] has prepared the CuCo2S4 composite and employed it as a CE in DSSC, which shows a better power conversion efficiency (PCE or η) of 7.56% than Pt (7.42%). However, it shows poor substrate adherence and conductivity. Carbon materials (such as Multiwalled Carbon Nanotubes (MWCNTs), reduced-Graphene Oxide (rGO), Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs), and others) have limited catalytic activity, whereas transition metal compounds have low conductivity due to their semiconductor characteristics [30,31]. In this scenario, a composite has been developed to improve catalytic activity and conductivity in comparison to single-component materials, allowing the PCE of DSSCs to be improved further [32]. Because of its excellent catalytic activity and stability, the transition metal (alloy) encapsulated (embedded) into carbon materials (CNTs, GQDs) system has received a lot of attention in the
I
3
−
reduction reaction [33]. Chang et al. [34] have developed a composite of CoS and GQDs and investigated its performance in the DSSC with a PCE of 7.30%, which is 24% higher than bare CoS CE. Apart from its performance in DSSCs, ternary sulfides have shown promising outcomes in supercapacitors and batteries in recent years [35]. Gentian et.al., have reported hedgehog ball structure of MoIn2S4 @CNTs CE via facile one step hydrothermal method in DSSC gained a photoelectric conversion efficiency of 8.38%, much higher than that of the DSSCs with MoIn2S4 (7.44%) and Pt (8.01%) CEs [36]. Similarly, Johnpeter et al. developed a DSSC that used CoSbS and CoSbS/MWCNT as the CEs and achieved a PCE of 4.4% and 6.0% respectively [37]. Supercapacitors (SCs) can store a large amount of energy and then discharge it when required via faradic and non-faradic chemical reactions. The energy storage process in battery-type electrodes is governed by the Nernstian process (Non-capacitive Faradic process), in which the transfer of localized valance electrons regulates the redox reaction. Whereas, the charging of a capacitive-type electrode is accomplished by separating ions at the electrode/electrolyte interface. Supercapatteries are a very new domain derived from supercapacitors and batteries [38]. TMS is distinguished from transition metal oxides by its high porous nature, rich redox reaction, rapid ion transport, short ion diffusion paths, and large charging/discharging cycles, which contribute to the electrode's energy density and stability [39]. Compared to equivalent oxide source materials, metallic sulfide-type based materials typically have higher power capabilities in conductivity, relatively low electronegativity, and a low band gap [40,41–46]. And though CuCo2S4 is regarded as a remarkable electrode material with high electrochemical properties, it has poor cycling stability and is prone to aggregation. As a result, it cannot make complete contact with the electrolyte, resulting in a decrease in electrochemical performance. Moreover, CuCo2S4 has been combined with other materials to fabricate nanostructured composites to reduce CuCo2S4 agglomeration [47]. Carbon nanomaterials have been extensively researched as electrode materials for supercapacitors due to their high electrical conductivity, thermal stability, and low cost [48,49]. Carbon-based nanomaterials can provide a viable charge transport pathway, which improves the performance rate and power density [50,51]. CCS-based composites are made by using a hydrothermal technique in this investigation, and the fundamental characterization is examined and reported. It will be utilized as a CE to investigate the performance of DSSCs, and the same material will be employed as a working electrode in supercapacitor applications.

This study uses a simple hydrothermal technique to synthesize CCS nanoparticles. Briefly, solution-A contains 0.2 M copper (II) nitrate trihydrate (Cu(NO3)2.3 H2O) and 0.2 M cobalt nitrate monohydrate (Co(NO3)2.xH2O) diluted in 30 mL deionized water (DI). In Solution B, 0.7 M of Thiourea (CH4N2S) is dissolved in 30 mL DI water. Afterward, both solutions are mixed (Solution-C) and placed in an autoclave heated at 180 °C for 24 h. The resulting product is collected and cleaned several times with DI water and ethanol. The obtained black precipitate is dried at 80 °C for 12 h before being utilized for further analysis.

In the hydrothermal process, the GQD is synthesized using H2O2 as an etching agent and ammonia as an aid [28]. In brief, 10 mg of graphene sheet (GS) is dissolved in 30 mL DI water, and then ammonia solution is added until pH 10 is reached. Finally, 2 mL H2O2 is added while stirring. After an hour of continuous stirring, the mixture is ultra-sonicated for 30 min. The resulting solution has been transferred to an autoclave and subjected to a 5-hours hydrothermal treatment at 170 °C. The colloidal black substance is then collected and washed with DI water and Ethanol (Solution-D) for the removal of unreacted products.

The CCS/MWCNT/GQDs composite has been synthesized with minor modifications to the above procedure. The approach typically involves 10 mg of MWCNT to solution-C (CCS/MWCNT), subsequently by the synthesized GQDs solution (Solution–D) (CCS/MWCNT/GQDs). The resulting solution is hydrothermally treated for 16 h at 180 °C after additional 30 min of stirring. The black precipitates are recovered after cooling and washed with DI water and ethanol before being dried in the air at 80 °C.

Photoanode is prepared on a properly cleaned fluorine doped tin oxide (FTO) plate using soap solution, DI water and isopropyl alcohol [28]. Before depositing the TiO2 film, the TiO2 blocking layer is coated on FTO using 40 mM of titanium isopropoxide solution by a spin coating technique (1500 rpm for 30 s) and calcinated at 450ºC for 10 min. After that, the TiO2 nanopowder is deposited on the FTO and sensitized N719 as described in the ref [52].

The CE is prepared by drop-casting technique. Initially, the obtained materials are ultrasonically dispersed in isopropyl alcohol for 10 min, resulting in CCS composites slurry, which is subsequently drop cast on the precleaned FTO plates. Secondly, the obtained films are dried at 80ºC for 12 h in a vacuum oven. The Pt CE and the fabrication of DSSC are prepared by using the standard procedure [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of working electrode for supercapacitor
ce:section.ce:para: The graphite sheet is employed as an active material substrate and polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) is utilized as a binder. First, the active material and PTFE are combined in a 95:5 ratio and made into a slurry with 10 μL of ethanol. The slurry is then coated on a 1 cm2 graphite sheet and dried at around 80 °C. The prepared electrode is used as a working electrode in the three electrode system [28].

The CCS, CCS/MWCNT and CCS/MWCNT/GQD composites are examined using a Shimadzu XRD-6000 diffractometer equipped with Fe Kα radiation. The particle distribution and morphology of the prepared composites are analyzed by Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM, TESCAN VEGA-3 LMU). Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) (JEOL-2100 +) identifies the shape and lattice dimension. The elements present in prepared composites are confirmed by X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS, PHI - VERSAPROBE III). The Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) and Tafel polarization performances are conducted by the electrochemical workstation system (CHI 660 D). The CV tests are performed using a three-electrode system that consists of synthesized CCS, a saturated Ag/AgCl electrode, and a platinum electrode that serves as the electrode, reference electrode, and counter electrode, respectively. The CV electrolyte is made up of 0.1 M LiClO4, 5 mM LiI, and 0.5 mM I2 in acetonitrile. The photovoltaic performance of the fabricated DSSCs are investigated using a solar simulator with a 150 W Xe light source in combination with standard AM1.5 (100 mW/cm2).

Fig. 1 reveals the XRD pattern of CCS, CCS/MWCNT, and CCS/MWCNT/GQD composites. The presence of a mixed phase is indicated by a careful examination of the powder XRD pattern. The analysis of the obtained product reveals that sharp peaks indicating high crystallinity with diffraction angles at 20.6, 33.2, 39.6, 48.3, 60.9, 64.1, and 70.71˚which corresponds to (111), (022), (113), (004), (115) and (044) planes of CuCo2S4, respectively (ICDD #42–1450) [29]. The two peaks of the diffraction angles at 22.8 and 28.1˚ marked by * , are matched with the orthorhombic Cu7S4 phase (ICDD #72–0617). Due to lower content, no additional peaks related to MWCNT and GQDs are visible. Furthermore, the addition of GQD to CCS/MWCNT composites causes no shift in peak positions; however, the spectrum shows a slight change in FWHM. Also, the determination of the texture coefficient includes details on the modified developments of the composite structure. Meanwhile, X-ray densities are estimated as a consequence of atomic structure factors. Every change in the atomic densities will lead to a shift in the texture coefficient of the associated Miller planes [12]. By this analysis, the pristine CCS are textured is a long (111) plane. However, when MWCNT/GQDs, and MWCNT are composited together, the texture coefficient of the (111) plane reduces about 18–23% value of the pristine CCS. Conversely, the texture coefficient along the dense (004) plane reduces, as evidenced by the integrated intensity ratio (I111/I004) of the planes (111) and (004).

The microstructure morphology of CCS, CCS/MWCNT and CCS/MWCNT/GQD are analyzed by SEM and TEM. The SEM images of synthesized CCS and CCS/MWCNT are shown in Fig. 2(a-b), where the CCS shows the spherical-like morphology and agglomerated spheres are observed for CCS/MWCNT. When GQD is introduced under ambient conditions, the shape of the microstructure is changed from a sphere to a flakes-like structure, as is evidenced by the texture coefficient estimation. The observation of the morphological transformation is evidenced in Fig. 2(c). The corresponding TEM images of the CCS/MWCNT/GQD composite are shown in Fig. 2(d,e), which shows that the GQDs are enveloped on the surface of the CCS nanostructures. The regions highlighted on the micrograph comprehend the creation and existence of GQDs on the surface of CCS/MWCNT (Fig. 2(f)). Fig. 2(g) illustrates the confirmation of the crystalline nature of the CCS/MWCNT/GQD through the SAED profile. Fig. 2(e) further shows that the CCS/MWCNT/GQD has a lattice distance of 0.29 nm (113), whereas the GQD has a lattice distance of 0.24 nm, which is precise with ICDD #42–1450.

The elemental content and oxidation state of the elements detected in the CCS/MWCNT/GQD composites are examined using XPS. The core-level spectrum of Co 2p, Cu 2p, S 2p, C 1 s and O 1 s are shown in Fig. 3(a-e), and the peaks are fitted using the Gaussian profile fitting. The spin-orbit doublets and two satellite peaks (represented as sat.) are observed in Fig. 3(a) which corresponds to the peak of Co 2p. Two prominent peaks at binding energies of 781.2 eV and 777.3 eV correspond to Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2, respectively [53], shown in Fig. 3(a). Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 have peaks at 930.8 eV and 951.4 eV, indicating the presence of Cu (I) and Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 have Cu(II) oxidation state peaks of 933.7 eV and 953.4 eV, respectively (Fig. 3(b)) [54–56]. Fig. 3(c) shows the S 2p spectra, where the two characteristic peaks around 161.3 eV and 168.8 eV correspond to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 respectively [57]. Fig. 3(d) displays the C 1 s scan spectrum, revealing three distinct peaks at CC at 283.8 eV, COH at 284.8 eV, C-O-C at 287.1 eV, indicating abundant carbon structure [58]. Furthermore, the peak of O 1 s shows the binding energy, which can be fitted into four peaks at 529.4, 530.1, 531.74, and 532.2 eV, respectively, for C-OH, CO, C-O-C, and C-O (Fig. 3(e)) [59].

Cyclic voltammetry (CV), Tafel polarization, and electrochemical impedance studies are used to investigate the electrocatalytic properties of the CEs with
I
3
−
ions in
I
−
/
I
3
−
redox couple. CV curves are recorded to compare the electrocatalytic activity of prepared CEs in the potential window − 0.4–1.4 V, with an electrolyte comprising 0.1 M LiClO4, 5 mM LiI, and 0.5 mM I2 in acetonitrile using a three-electrode system at sweep rate 50 mVs−1. Fig. 4(a) shows, the CV spectra of CEs with CCS, CCS/MWCNT, and CCS/MWCNT/GQD show a pair of oxidations (right side) and reduction peaks (left side). According to earlier literature studies, the Red-1 peak is attributed to the reduction of I3 - to I-, whereas the Ox-1 peak is attributed to the oxidation of I- to I3 -. While I2 to I3 - reduction is responsible for the Red-2 peak, I3 - to I2 oxidation is the cause of the Ox-2 peak [60]. The following Eq. (1) and (2) represent the redox reaction at the electrolyte/electrode interface.
(1)
I
3
−
+ 2
e
−
⇌ 3
I
−
(2)
3
I
−
+ 2
e
−
⇌ 2
I
3
−

In general, the peak-to-peak potential separation (EPP) between Red-1 and Ox-1and peak current density (JPC) are the important factors influencing electrocatalytic activity. The smaller EPP value and the higher the JPC value are crucial parameters for the better reversibility of the redox reaction and the faster reduction velocity. When GQDs are incorporated into the CCS/MWCNT catalyst, the cathodic and anodic peak current increases and EPP between cathodic and anodic peaks decreases simultaneously, indicating the carbon materials may enhance the conductivity of the CCS composite [61,62]. The estimated EPP values of CCS, CCS/MWCNT and CCS/MWCNT/GQDCEs are 0.358 V, 0.328 V, and 0.292 V, respectively. Similarly, the JPC values of the CCS/MWCNT/GQD CE have a higher value (3.4 mA cm−2) than CCS (1.9 mA cm−2), CCS/MWCNTCEs (2.9 mA cm−2). On the other hand, low EPP and high JPC values reveal that the microstructure CCS/MWCNT/GQD CE is a significant catalyst for reducing
I
3
−
, making it a better alternative to Pt CEs in DSSCs.

Fig. 4(b) represents the obtained Tafel polarization curve of the CCS, CCS/MWCNT and CCS/MWCNT/GQDCEs based symmetric dummy cell should reveal the interfacial charge transfer properties. The polarization zone (curve at low potential), the Tafel zone (middle potential with sharp slope), and the diffusion zone (curve at high potential) are the three critical zones of the Tafel curve [63]. Based on the earlier studies of Chen et.al. [64], the limiting diffusion current density (Jlim) has been calculated by the intersection at the y-axis of the Tafel curve, whereas the exchange current density (J0) can be obtained from the intersection of the linear line at 0 V with the tangent line from the polarization curve. Jlim indicates the diffusion velocity of
I
−
/
I
3
−
in the electrolyte, whereas J0 represents the reduction activity of
I
3
−
. Jlim has a positive relationship with the diffusion coefficient (D), (illustrating the redox couple's diffusion character in the electrolyte) based on Eq.(3).
(3)
J
lim
=
2 neDc
N
A
l
Where l is the spacer thickness, c is the
I
3
−
concentration, NA is Avogadro's constant, and e and n have their usual meanings. Furthermore, the Charge Transfer Resistance (RCT) corresponding to the electrode/electrolyte interface can be used to calculate the exchange current density (J0) of an electrocatalytic film (Eq.(4)).
(4)
J
o
=
RT
nF
R
CT

Based on the results obtained, Jlim for CCS/MWCNT/GQD shows the highest Jlim of all other CEs, indicating the CCS/MWCNT/GQD has the best performance in the diffusion velocity of
I
−
/
I
3
−
compared to CCS and CCS/MWCNT CEs. From the Tafel curve, the estimated J0 for all CEs is in the order of CCS/MWCNT/GQD > CCS/MWCNT > CCS CEs. These results show that the CCS/MWCNT/GQD is the best CE for reducing tri-iodide compared to other CEs due to the synergetic effect of the materials.

Fig. 5(a) depicts the photocurrent density and voltage (J-V) spectra of fabricated DSSC based on CCS, CCS/MWCNT, CCS/MWCNT/GQD and Pt CEs. The key parameters like open-circuit voltage (VOC), short circuit current density (JSC), Fill Factor (FF) and PCE are calculated from J-V spectra and are summarized in Table (1). Fig. 5(a) demonstrates that all modified cells perform better than the pristine cells (CCS). With CCS/MWCNT/GQD as CE, PCE of (4.15%) is achieved which exceeds the PCE of CCS/MWCNT (3.09%) and CCS (1.91%). The photovoltaic characteristics of CCS/MWCNT/GQD CE are VOC of 0.76 V, JSC of 11.39 mA cm−2, FF of 0.46 and η of 4.15%. It is very attractively to note that the value of JSC is enhanced in the CCS/MWCNT/GQD CE device, which may attribute low recombination rate, increasing the injection ability of photogenerated electrons and the synergetic catalytic effect between CCS, MWCNT and GQD. The value of FF is also get increased due to the low Series Resistance (RS) obtained in the impedance spectra. The exceeding performance achieved for CCS/MWCNT/GQD composite is due to the adding little GQD on CCS/MWCNT surface triggering the fast charge transfer rate between CCS/electrolyte interface, increasing electrical conductivity which is consisted of the CV and Tafel polarization spectra. Furthermore, the superior electrocatalytic nature is ascribed due to the synergetic effect of MWCNT and CCS where synergy of MWCNT enables a fine conductivity and CCS offers a excellent catalytic activity, causing an increase in the PCE [65,66]. However, the obtained PCE of this work is significantly lower than that of previously reported works. This is because the photoanode is thicker (∼ 20 µm), and thus carrier recombination is high at the photoanode/dye interface, distorting the cell's PCE. If we can overcome this, we may get better results.

To reduce the impact of the photoanode and better understand the charge transfer processes at the interface between the electrolyte and the CE, we have investigated the electrochemical impedance spectra using a symmetric sandwich-type structure (Fig. 5(b)). Each profile involves two well-defined semicircles [67]. The semicircle at the starting point of the high-frequency region is used to calculate the RS of the interfacial resistance between the electrode materials and the FTO conducting glass substrate. The RCT at the CE and electrolyte interface is represented by the first semicircle at high frequency, while the second semicircle represents the Nernst diffusion resistance (ZN) within the electrolyte at a lower frequency. For DSSC using CCS, CCS/MWCNT and CCS/MWCNT/GQD as CEs, the value of RS is 12.4, 5.7 and 3.5 Ω and the values of RCT are 160.9, 115.1 and 26.8 Ω respectively. It can be noticed that RCT values of CCS/MWCNT/GQD CE are smaller than that of other CEs, maintaining an acceleration of the tri-iodide reduction attributed to the higher catalytic activity of CCS/MWCNT/GQD CE.

In Fig. 6, the Incident Photon to Current Conversion Efficiency (IPCE) of the prepared CEs is plotted against wavelength. The IPCEs of all of the cells are highest at around 520 nm, which corresponds to the N719 dye's maximum absorption. CCS/MWCNT/GQD CE obtained the maximum IPCE of 21% between 400 and 650 nm, which is higher than the other CE materials. The enhancement of the IPCE is due to incorporating carbon materials (MWCNTs and GQDs) on the CCS surface. This enhancement is due to the reduction in carrier recombination and enhancement in the harvesting capacity.

The supercapacitive behavior of the prepared CCS, CCS/MWCNT and CCS/MWCNT/GQD electrodes have been determined using CV and Galvanostatic Charge–Discharge tests (GCD) and a.c. Impedance analysis. Fig. 7(a-b) depicts the CV graph for prepared electrodes within the potential window from − 0.6–0.3 V in 6 M KOH electrolyte. As clearly indicated by the larger area under the CV curve for CCS/MWCNT/GQD than CCS and CCS/MWCNT, one can easily conclude that the improvement in electrochemical performance is further indicated later in terms of the higher capacitance of CCS/MWCNT/GQD. Further, the obtained CV curve has a strong redox peak, implying that the electrochemical capacitance is primarily due to redox reactions. There is also noticeable reversible charge transport, implying that the material is capacitive. The redox peak in the CV plots is primarily due to redox reactions related to the Co2+/Cu2+ redox couple, which are possibly arbitrated by the OH- ions in the electrolyte.

Moreover, the CV curve for CCS/MWCNT/GQD electrode is studied at different scan rates (Fig. 7(b)) and it shows that the nature of the CV curve gets changes when increasing the scan rate and observing the anodic and cathodic peaks shift slightly towards negative and positive directions. This shift in peaks can be attributed to polarizability, electrode conductivity, internal resistance, higher power character, and excellent electrochemical reversibility [68,69]. The GCD behavior of synthesized CCS composites is investigated in a potential window of − 0.6–0.3 V at current density 2 A g−1(vs. Ag/AgCl), which is depicted in Fig. 7(c). The GCD spectra of the CCS-based electrode (Fig. 7(c)) show a sloping plateau, i.e., a combined state of capacitance behavior with a regular triangular profile and ending with capacity (battery-like action) with flat discharge curve. This is due to the electrode's mixed pseudocapacitive/battery behavior, which is linked to the layered structure of CCS composites, which allows electrolyte diffusion and ionic species intercalation[70–73]. The specific capacity (CS.) of the CCS-based electrodes can be estimated using Eq.(5).
(5)
C
S
=
I ∆ T
m
Where CS is the specific capacity, I is the applied current, m is the mass of the active material[11]. Fig. 7(d) demonstrates the GCD spectra of CCS/MWCNT/GQD electrode at the specific currents of 2, 4, 6, 8 and 10 Ag−1 respectively. The fading of specific capacitance is observed as the current density increases, which could be explained by an increase in the internal resistance of the electrode [74]. At higher current densities, the electrode material's rate performance is significantly reduced, owing to insufficient time for electrolyte ion intercalation. Based on the time interval, the specific capacity is estimated. At a specific current of 2 A g−1, it is found to be 175 C g−1, 226 C g−1 and 402 C g−1 for CCS, CCS/MWCNT and CCS/MWCNT/GQD respectively. For CCS/MWCNT/GQD the specific capacity has been calculated based on the discharge curve and it was found to be 1395 C g−1 and 607 C g−1 at 0.05 and 1 A g−1. The GCD measurements also show that the GQDs incorporated CCS/MWCNT composite performs better in supercapacitors by acquiring a larger area (Fig. 7(d)) of the discharge curve, resulting in a 54.8% higher capacity than pristine CCS. The GQD included CCS/MWCNT electrode exhibits significant electrocatalytic activity in iodine electrolyte as a result of its wide surface area, and as a result, the CCS/MWCNT/GQD based DSSC device also yields high PCE. The CCS/MWCNT/GQD electrode performs well in KOH as well as iodine electrolyte, one can infer from this. The acquired result is evaluated against the newly released ternary metal sulfide-based works shown in Table 2. An electrochemical impedance technique has been used to understand the electrochemical behavior of the CCS-based composites, which is shown in Fig. 7(e) and the obtained data is fitted to Randle's equivalent circuit. The calculated RCT values for CCS, CCS/MWCNT and CCS/MWCNT/GQD are 1.18, 2.98, 10.1 Ω, respectively. The CCS/MWCNT/GQD shows the low RCT value may be due to increases the OH- insertion into the material that results from the excellent electrochemical performance, which is strongly consistent with the GCD performance. In addition, the symmetric supercapacitor's cycle life is tested over 5000 continuous charge/discharge cycles at a much higher current density of 20 A g−1 to determine the electrodes' practical utility. The end of the results shows that the 89.2% capacity has been retained even after 5000 cycles (Fig. 7(f)).

Additionally, a two-terminal device (supercapacitor) is more realistic in application circumstances. CCS/MWCNT/GQD is used as one electrode and MWCNT as the other in the sandwich design hybrid supercapacitor. A separator made of thin polyurethane foam that had been soaked in 0.5 M KOH was used (as an electrolyte). Fig. 8(a-d) demonstrate several electrochemical analyses of a hybrid supercapacitor, including CV, GCD, and Nyquist plot respectively. From CV, it has been observed that the charge storage mechanism is completely faradic as shown in Fig. 8(a). Moreover, the area of the CV curve is frequently enhanced by increasing the scan rate 25, 50, 75, 100 mV s−1. By using the GCD technique, the specific capacitance are calculated for various current densities at constant window (0–1.6 V) (Fig. 8(b)). The specific capacitance (F g−1) derived from GCD measurements is ‘Cs’, and the discharge time is ‘t′. Using the relationships from the discharge curves, the energy density (W h kg−1) and power density (W kg−1) of the CCS/MWCNT/GQD based electrodes are also calculated.
(6)
E =
1 2
C
s
V
2
(7)
P =
E
Δ T

At a constant current density of 2 A g−1, it gives an energy density of 109.6 W h kg−1 and an ultra-high power density of 5302 W kg−1 (Fig. 8(c)). Using the Nyquist plot, the RCT can be calculated between the electrode/electrolyte interface which shows in Fig. 8(d). As is well known that a lower RCT enhances ion insertion into the material, which leads to better electrochemical performance.

Facile hydrothermal treatment is used to synthesize the CCS, CCS/MWCNT and CCS/MWCNT/GQD nanostructures and is employed as a catalyst in DSSC and supercapacitor. The XRD profile confirms the formation of CuCo2S4 composites. The presence of GQDs on CCS/MWCNT surface is observed through SEM. XPS examines the elemental composition and chemical states. The flake-like CCS/MWCNT/GQD CE shows superior electrocatalytic activity against tri-iodide reduction on the CE side, which is consistent with the CV and Tafel polarization studies. The lower RCT of the CCS/MWCNT/GQD CE reveals a rapid I3 - reduction between the CE/electrolyte interfaces than other proposed CEs, which consisted of the results of electrochemical studies. The performance of GQDs embedded CCS/MWCNT CE-based DSSC device exhibited better power conversion efficiency (4.15%), 54% higher than bare CCS CE (1.91%). Further, the prepared nanostructures are employed as working electrodes in three-electrode systems and the energy storage performance is investigated. Electrochemical studies reveal that the combined effect of GQDs and CCS results in a remarkable capacitive performance (402 C g−1 at 2 A g-1) and shows 11% of capacitive loss after 5000 charge/discharge cycles. As a result, the CCS nanostructure supported by MWCNT and GQDs is an excellent electrode material for energy conversion and storage gadgets.

V. Gayathri: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. E. Praveen: Resources, Formal analysis. K. Jayakumar: Methodology. S. Karazhanov: Review & editing. C. Raja Mohan: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there are no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank BT corp for providing MWCNT (CAS No: 308068-56-6).
